mnemonics.title: State Meaning in Pattern Matching
mnemonics.content: State q = Length of longest prefix of pattern that is suffix of input processed so far. When state = m (pattern length), a match is found!

mnemonics.title: DFA vs NFA for Text Search
mnemonics.content: DFA: Deterministic - one state at a time → O(n) time. NFA: Non-deterministic - multiple states → O(mn) worst case. DFA = Deterministic FAster, NFA = Non-deterministic (slower but flexible)

mnemonics.title: Myhill-Nerode for Pattern Matching
mnemonics.content: For pattern length m, minimal DFA has m+1 states. States 0,1,2,...,m represent matching 0,1,2,...,m characters of pattern as suffix.

acronyms.expansion: Knuth-Morris-Pratt - builds failure function to simulate DFA, achieves O(m+n) time
acronyms.usage: When you need linear-time single pattern matching with preprocessing

acronyms.expansion: Nondeterministic Finite Automaton - can be in multiple states simultaneously, useful for regex-based pattern construction
acronyms.usage: Constructing pattern matcher from regular expressions

acronyms.expansion: Deterministic Finite Automaton - one transition per symbol per state, O(n) text search
acronyms.usage: When optimal search speed is required after preprocessing

acronyms.expansion: Kleene star - set of all strings (including empty) over alphabet Σ
acronyms.usage: In regex Σ*PΣ* means any string containing pattern P

visualTips.visualization: Draw states as circles labeled 0 to m. Show transitions for each alphabet symbol as arrows. Accepting states (typically state m) are double-circled. The diagram shows how the automaton 'slides' over text.

visualTips.visualize: Think of the text as a stream flowing through the automaton. The automaton 'accepts' the text when it enters the accepting state (pattern found). The key insight: we're checking if text belongs to language Σ*PΣ*.

analogies.analogy: The automaton state is like remembering just enough of what you've seen to know if the pattern could start now. Like remembering the last few characters that matter for a potential match.

Think of the DFA as a lock with m+1 positions. Each character of text is a key that either advances the lock toward position m (matching) or resets it. The lock position tells you how much of the pattern you've matched.
